Ignoring divergence - the most reliable signal
Using wrong EMA period for timeframe
Using Elder-Ray alone without trend context
Expecting exact reversals at extremes

faq.q: What is the best Elder-Ray period for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, 13-period EMA works for swing trading. For day trading, use 9-period. For Bitcoin, 13-period is standard. Altcoins may need 9-period for faster signals.

faq.q: How to use Elder-Ray with MACD?
faq.a: Use MACD for momentum and Elder-Ray for power confirmation. When both bullish, trend is strong. When they diverge, potential reversal.

faq.q: What does it mean when Bull Power and Bear Power both positive?
faq.a: Both positive means buyers are strong enough to push price above EMA, and sellers can't push below it. This is a powerful uptrend signal.

meta.title: Accumulation Distribution - A/D Line: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Accumulation Distribution formula, volume flow analysis, money flow signals, divergence patterns, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Accumulation Distribution: Complete A/D Line Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Accumulation Distribution (A/D) Line tracks cumulative money flow. Rising A/D Line indicates accumulation (buying pressure), falling indicates distribution (selling pressure). Divergence between A/D Line and price often signals reversals.
key_takeaways: Rising A/D = accumulation (buying)
Falling A/D = distribution (selling)
Divergence from price = reversal signal
A/D leads price in many cases

people_also_ask.question: What is the difference between A/D and OBV?
people_also_ask.a: A/D uses close position within the range (multiplying by volume). OBV simply adds/subtracts volume based on up/down days. A/D is more sensitive to intraday price movements.

people_also_ask.question: How to use A/D Line with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use A/D for volume confirmation and RSI for momentum. When A/D rising and RSI oversold, accumulation is happening - strong buy signal.

people_also_ask.question: What does A/D divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence: price lower low, A/D higher low - accumulation despite lower prices. Bearish divergence: price higher high, A/D lower high - distribution despite higher prices.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Accumulation Distribution Line shows whether money is flowing into or out of a stock. Think of it as a volume-powered trend confirmation tool. Rising A/D = smart money buying (accumulation). Falling A/D = smart money selling (distribution). Divergence from price is the most powerful signal.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Money Flow Multiplier = ((Close - Low) - (High - Close)) / (High - Low)
Money Flow Volume = Money Flow Multiplier × Volume
Accumulation Distribution = Cumulative sum of Money Flow Volume

rows.meaning: Accumulation happening - money flowing in. Bullish.
Distribution happening - money flowing out. Bearish.
Strong buying pressure. Price likely to follow higher.
Strong selling pressure. Price likely to follow lower.
Price and A/D moving opposite. Reversal likely ahead.

required.title: Accumulation Distribution Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Accumulation Distribution Works
recommended.content: A/D Line calculates money flow based on the close's position within the daily range. When close is in the upper part of the range, money flows in (accumulation). When close is in the lower part, money flows out (distribution). The cumulative line shows whether buying or selling pressure is dominant over time. A/D often leads price, making it a valuable leading indicator.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Accumulation Distribution Calculation
items: Calculate Money Flow Multiplier: ((Close-Low) - (High-Close)) / (High-Low)
Multiply by Volume to get Money Flow Volume
Add Money Flow Volume to previous A/D value
Positive values = accumulation, negative = distribution
Plot cumulative line
Watch for divergence between A/D and price

items.name: A/D Trend Confirmation
items.description: Price rising with A/D rising = strong uptrend. Price rising with A/D falling = weak uptrend. Use for trend confirmation.

items.name: A/D Divergence Strategy
items.description: Bullish divergence: price lower low, A/D higher low = buy. Bearish divergence: price higher high, A/D lower high = sell.

items.name: A/D + Volume Strategy
items.description: A/D rising with volume increasing = strong accumulation. A/D falling with volume increasing = strong distribution.

items.name: A/D + Support/Resistance Strategy
items.description: Watch A/D at support/resistance levels. A/D divergence at key levels gives stronger reversal signals.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
cons: Less effective in low volume markets

optional.title: History
optional.content: Accumulation Distribution was developed by Marc Chaikin as an improvement over OBV. Chaikin's innovation was using the close's position within the range to determine money flow direction. The A/D Line is a core component of Chaikin's trading system.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: A/D Line works best in liquid markets with consistent volume. It's particularly effective in stocks and crypto. Use with ADX for trend confirmation.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using A/D alone without price confirmation
Ignoring divergence - the most reliable signal
Using A/D on low volume markets
Expecting exact price targets from A/D
Using A/D without volume data

faq.q: How to use A/D with MACD?
faq.a: Use MACD for momentum and A/D for volume confirmation. When both bullish, trend is strong. A/D divergence with MACD gives high probability reversal signals.

faq.q: What is the difference between A/D and Chaikin Money Flow?
faq.a: A/D is cumulative (running total). CMF is smoothed over periods. A/D shows long-term accumulation, CMF shows shorter-term money flow.

faq.q: Does A/D work in crypto?
faq.a: Yes, A/D works in crypto markets with reliable volume data. A/D divergence is effective for catching Bitcoin and altcoin reversals.

meta.title: Ultimate Oscillator - Multi-Timeframe: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Ultimate Oscillator formula, multi-timeframe momentum, overbought/oversold signals, divergence patterns, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Ultimate Oscillator: Complete Multi-Timeframe Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Ultimate Oscillator combines short, medium, and long-term momentum using weighted averages. Readings above 70 indicate overbought, below 30 indicate oversold. The multi-timeframe approach reduces false signals compared to single-timeframe oscillators.
key_takeaways: Above 70 = overbought (potential sell)
Below 30 = oversold (potential buy)
Multi-timeframe reduces false signals
Divergence signals are highly reliable

people_also_ask.question: What is the best Ultimate Oscillator setting for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard settings are (7,14,28). Day traders often use (5,10,20) for faster signals. Swing traders stick with standard settings.

people_also_ask.question: How is Ultimate Oscillator different from RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Ultimate Oscillator uses multiple timeframes (7,14,28) with different weights. RSI uses a single timeframe. UO reduces false signals from single-period analysis.

people_also_ask.question: What are the weights in Ultimate Oscillator?
people_also_ask.a: Weights are: Short-term (7) = 4, Medium-term (14) = 2, Long-term (28) = 1. This gives more importance to shorter-term momentum while still considering longer-term context.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Ultimate Oscillator uses three timeframes (7, 14, 28) to reduce false signals. Think of it as RSI with multiple lenses. Above 70 = overbought. Below 30 = oversold. The multi-timeframe approach gives more reliable signals than single-period oscillators.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
BP = Close - Min(Low, Previous Close)
TR = Max(High, Previous Close) - Min(Low, Previous Close)
Average7 = Sum(BP,7) / Sum(TR,7)
Average14 = Sum(BP,14) / Sum(TR,14)
Average28 = Sum(BP,28) / Sum(TR,28)
UO = (4×Avg7 + 2×Avg14 + 1×Avg28) / (4+2+1) × 100

rows.meaning: Multi-timeframe overbought - potential sell. More reliable than RSI alone.
Multi-timeframe oversold - potential buy. More reliable than RSI alone.
Bullish - leaving oversold. Potential buy signal.
Bearish - leaving overbought. Potential sell signal.
Multi-timeframe divergence. Very reliable reversal signal.

required.title: Ultimate Oscillator Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Ultimate Oscillator Works
recommended.content: Ultimate Oscillator calculates Buying Pressure (BP) and True Range (TR) for each period. It then averages these over three timeframes (7, 14, 28) with different weights. Short-term (7) gets weight 4, medium (14) gets weight 2, long (28) gets weight 1. This weighted average reduces false signals by considering multiple timeframes simultaneously.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Ultimate Oscillator Calculation
items: Calculate BP: Close - Min(Low, Previous Close)
Calculate TR: Max(High, Previous Close) - Min(Low, Previous Close)
Average BP/TR for 7, 14, and 28 periods
Apply weights: 4×Avg7 + 2×Avg14 + 1×Avg28
Divide by sum of weights (7) and multiply by 100
Read overbought/oversold at 70/30

items.name: Overbought/Oversold Strategy
items.description: Buy when UO crosses above 30 (leaving oversold). Sell when UO crosses below 70 (leaving overbought). More reliable than RSI.

items.name: Divergence Strategy
items.description: Bullish divergence: price lower low, UO higher low = buy. Bearish divergence: price higher high, UO lower high = sell. Very reliable.

items.name: UO + Trend Strategy
items.description: Combine with ADX. Use UO signals when ADX > 25. Avoid UO signals in sideways markets. Higher win rate.

items.name: UO + Price Action Strategy
items.description: Look for reversal candlesticks at UO extremes. Pin bars or engulfing patterns at 70/30 with UO confirmation = high probability.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
pros: More reliable than RSI or Stochastic alone
cons: Can still give false signals in strong trends

optional.title: History
optional.content: Ultimate Oscillator was developed by Larry Williams in 1985. Williams wanted to create an oscillator that reduced false signals from single-timeframe analysis. He introduced the multi-timeframe approach with weighted averages. The indicator was published in Technical Analysis of Stocks & Commodities magazine.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: Ultimate Oscillator works in all market conditions but excels in trending markets where momentum is clear. In sideways markets, use with caution. Combine with ADX for trend confirmation.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Trading every overbought/oversold signal without context
Ignoring divergence - the most reliable signal
Using default settings without testing
Using UO alone without trend confirmation
Expecting exact reversals at extremes

faq.q: What is the best Ultimate Oscillator setting for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, standard (7,14,28) works well. For day trading, use (5,10,20). For Bitcoin, standard settings. Altcoins may need (7,14,21) for better signals.

faq.q: How to use Ultimate Oscillator with MACD?
faq.a: Use MACD for trend and UO for entry signals. Enter when UO shows overbought/oversold and MACD confirms trend direction. Stronger signals with both agreeing.

faq.q: What is the difference between Ultimate Oscillator and RSI?
faq.a: UO uses three timeframes (7,14,28) with weights. RSI uses a single timeframe (typically 14). UO reduces false signals by considering multiple timeframes.

meta.title: RVI - Relative Vigor Index: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn RVI formula, vigor measurement, trend strength analysis, crossover signals, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: RVI: Complete Relative Vigor Index Guide

aeo.direct_answer: RVI (Relative Vigor Index) measures the vigor of price movement using open, high, low, and close. RVI above zero indicates bullish momentum, below zero indicates bearish momentum. Crossovers between RVI and its signal line give trading signals.
key_takeaways: Above 0 = bullish momentum
Below 0 = bearish momentum
Crossover = entry signal
Uses OHLC data for accuracy

people_also_ask.question: What is the best RVI setting for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard setting is 10-period. Day traders often use 7-period for faster signals. Swing traders use 14-period for smoother readings.

people_also_ask.question: How is RVI different from RSI?
people_also_ask.a: RVI uses OHLC data (open, high, low, close) to measure vigor. RSI only uses closing prices. RVI gives a more complete picture of price action.

people_also_ask.question: What does RVI divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence: price lower low, RVI higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, RVI lower high. Both signal potential reversals.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: RVI measures the vigor of price movement using open, high, low, and close. Think of it as a comprehensive momentum indicator. Above zero = bullish vigor. Below zero = bearish vigor. Crossovers with its signal line give entry signals. RVI uses more data than RSI for better accuracy.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Numerator = (Close - Open) + 2 × (Close - Open)_1 + 2 × (Close - Open)_2 + (Close - Open)_3
Denominator = (High - Low) + 2 × (High - Low)_1 + 2 × (High - Low)_2 + (High - Low)_3
RVI = SMA(Numerator, N) / SMA(Denominator, N)
Signal Line = SMA(RVI, 4)

rows.meaning: Bullish vigor - price momentum up. Buyers in control.
Bearish vigor - price momentum down. Sellers in control.
Bullish crossover - momentum turning up. Potential buy.
Bearish crossover - momentum turning down. Potential sell.
Price and RVI moving opposite. Reversal likely ahead.

recommended.title: How RVI Works
recommended.content: RVI uses all four OHLC prices to measure price vigor. It compares the change from open to close (vigor) against the high-low range (volatility). The indicator uses a weighted average of these changes over multiple periods. A signal line (4-period SMA) provides crossover signals. RVI gives a more complete picture of momentum than price-only indicators.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step RVI Calculation
items: Calculate numerator: weighted sum of (Close - Open)
Calculate denominator: weighted sum of (High - Low)
Average both over N periods using SMA
RVI = Numerator_Avg / Denominator_Avg
Calculate Signal Line: SMA of RVI over 4 periods
Watch for crossovers between RVI and Signal Line

items.name: Crossover Strategy
items.description: Buy when RVI crosses above signal line. Sell when RVI crosses below signal line. Simple and effective in trending markets.

items.name: Zero Line Strategy
items.description: Buy when RVI crosses above zero (bullish). Sell when RVI crosses below zero (bearish). Trend direction confirmation.

items.name: Divergence Strategy
items.description: Bullish divergence: price lower low, RVI higher low = buy. Bearish divergence: price higher high, RVI lower high = sell.

items.name: RVI + ADX Strategy
items.description: Use ADX to confirm trend. Take RVI signals only when ADX > 25. Avoid signals in sideways markets.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
pros: Uses all OHLC data - more complete picture
cons: Can be noisy in sideways markets

optional.title: History
optional.content: Relative Vigor Index was developed by John Ehlers in 2002. Ehlers, a pioneer in technical analysis, created RVI to improve on RSI by incorporating all OHLC data. The indicator provides a more comprehensive measure of price momentum than close-only indicators.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: RVI works best in trending markets with clear momentum. In sideways markets, crossovers can be noisy. Combine with ADX for best results.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Trading every crossover without confirmation
Ignoring divergence signals
Using RVI alone without other indicators
Using wrong period for timeframe
Expecting RVI to work in all conditions

faq.q: What is the best RVI period for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, 10-period RVI works for swing trading. For day trading, use 7-period. For Bitcoin, 10-period is standard. Altcoins may need 7-period for faster signals.

faq.q: How to use RVI with MACD?
faq.a: Use MACD for trend and RVI for momentum confirmation. When both bullish, trend is strong. RVI diverging from MACD signals potential reversals.

faq.q: What is the difference between RVI and RSI?
faq.a: RVI uses all OHLC prices to measure vigor. RSI only uses closing prices. RVI gives a more complete picture of price momentum using the full range of price data.

meta.title: Aroon - Trend Strength: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Aroon formula, trend strength measurement, Aroon up/down signals, crossover patterns, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Aroon: Complete Trend Strength Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Aroon measures trend strength and direction using Aroon Up (time since new high) and Aroon Down (time since new low). Readings above 70 indicate strong trends. Crossovers between Aroon Up and Down signal trend changes.
key_takeaways: Aroon Up > 70 = strong uptrend
Aroon Down > 70 = strong downtrend
Aroon Up crossing above Aroon Down = bullish
Aroon Down crossing above Aroon Up = bearish

people_also_ask.question: What is the best Aroon period for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard Aroon uses 25 periods. Day traders often use 14-period for faster signals. Swing traders use 25-period standard.

people_also_ask.question: How to use Aroon with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use Aroon for trend strength and RSI for momentum. When Aroon shows uptrend and RSI oversold, it's a strong buy signal.

people_also_ask.question: What does Aroon crossover indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Aroon Up crossing above Aroon Down indicates new uptrend starting. Aroon Down crossing above Aroon Up indicates new downtrend starting. The crossover signals trend changes.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Aroon measures trend strength using time since new highs and lows. Aroon Up shows how long since a new high. Aroon Down shows how long since a new low. Above 70 = strong trend. Crossovers = trend changes. Simple but effective for trend identification.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Aroon Up = ((N - Days Since New High) / N) × 100
Aroon Down = ((N - Days Since New Low) / N) × 100
Where N = 25 periods (standard)
Range: 0 to 100
Above 70 = strong, below 30 = weak

rows.meaning: Strong uptrend - new high recently made. Bullish.
Strong downtrend - new low recently made. Bearish.

rows.signal: Aroon Up crosses above Aroon Down
rows.meaning: Bullish crossover - uptrend starting. Potential buy.

rows.signal: Aroon Down crosses above Aroon Up
rows.meaning: Bearish crossover - downtrend starting. Potential sell.
No clear trend - ranging market. Avoid trend strategies.

recommended.title: How Aroon Works
recommended.content: Aroon measures the time since the most recent new high and new low over a period. If a new high was made recently, Aroon Up is high (strong uptrend). If a new low was made recently, Aroon Down is high (strong downtrend). The crossover between Aroon Up and Down signals trend changes. Aroon is unique in measuring time rather than price or volume.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Aroon Calculation
items: Find the highest high over N periods
Find the lowest low over N periods
Calculate days since each extreme
Aroon Up = ((N - Days Since High) / N) × 100
Aroon Down = ((N - Days Since Low) / N) × 100
Watch for crossovers and extreme readings

items.name: Crossover Strategy
items.description: Buy when Aroon Up crosses above Aroon Down. Sell when Aroon Down crosses above Aroon Up. Simple trend change signal.

items.name: Extreme Strategy
items.description: Enter when one Aroon line goes above 70 (strong trend). Exit when it falls below 50. Trend following with strength confirmation.

items.name: Aroon + ADX Strategy
items.description: Use ADX to confirm trend strength. Trade Aroon crossovers when ADX > 25. Avoid signals in weak trends.

items.name: Aroon + Pullback Strategy
items.description: In uptrend (Aroon Up > Aroon Down), buy when Aroon Up pulls back to 30-40 then rises again. Trend resumption signal.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
cons: Can give false signals in sideways markets

optional.title: History
optional.content: Aroon was developed by Tushar Chande in 1995. Chande, a technical analyst and author, created Aroon to measure trend strength based on time rather than price. 'Aroon' means 'dawn's early light' in Sanskrit, reflecting the indicator's role in identifying new trends early.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: Aroon works best in trending markets where new highs and lows are being made. In sideways markets, both lines stay below 30. Use with ADX to confirm trend strength.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using Aroon alone without confirmation
Trading every crossover without context
Using default 25-period for all markets
Ignoring when both lines are below 30 (no trend)
Expecting Aroon to work in range-bound markets

faq.q: What is the best Aroon period for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, 25-period Aroon works for swing trading. For day trading, use 14-period. For Bitcoin, 25-period is standard. Altcoins may need 14-period for faster signals.

faq.q: How to use Aroon with Bollinger Bands?
faq.a: Use Aroon for trend direction and Bollinger Bands for volatility. Trade Aroon signals when Bollinger Bands confirm volatility. Stronger signals when both agree.

faq.q: What does it mean when both Aroon lines are below 30?
faq.a: Both below 30 indicates no clear trend. The market is ranging or consolidating. Avoid trend-following strategies until one line moves above 50.

meta.title: Chaikin Volatility - Volatility Oscillator: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Chaikin Volatility formula, volatility measurement, breakout signals, volatility expansion/contraction, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Chaikin Volatility: Complete Volatility Oscillator Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Chaikin Volatility measures the rate of change of the high-low range over time. Increasing Chaikin Volatility indicates expanding volatility, decreasing indicates contracting volatility. The indicator helps identify potential breakouts and volatility cycles.
key_takeaways: Increasing = volatility expansion
Decreasing = volatility contraction
Low levels suggest potential breakout
High levels suggest potential exhaustion

people_also_ask.question: What is the best Chaikin Volatility period for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard settings are (10,20) for short and long EMAs. Day traders often use (5,10) for faster signals. Swing traders use (10,20) standard.

people_also_ask.question: How to use Chaikin Volatility with Bollinger Bands?
people_also_ask.a: Use Chaikin Volatility to confirm Bollinger Band squeeze signals. When bands squeeze and Chaikin Volatility is low, expect expansion. When Chaikin Volatility spikes, volatility is expanding.

people_also_ask.question: What does Chaikin Volatility divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Price making new highs while Chaikin Volatility making lower highs suggests weakening volatility and potential reversal. Divergence signals caution.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Chaikin Volatility measures how fast volatility is changing. Think of it as a volatility speedometer. Rising = volatility expanding (bigger moves ahead). Falling = volatility contracting (quiet period ahead). Low readings often precede breakouts.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
High-Low Range = High - Low
EMA_Range_Short = EMA(High-Low, N1)
EMA_Range_Long = EMA(High-Low, N2)
Chaikin Volatility = (EMA_Range_Short - EMA_Range_Long) / EMA_Range_Long × 100
Where N1 = 10, N2 = 20 (standard)

rows.meaning: Volatility expanding - bigger price swings ahead. Prepare for breakouts.
Volatility contracting - quieter market. Squeeze building.
Low volatility regime - breakout likely. Watch for direction.
High volatility regime - potential exhaustion. Trend may be overextended.
Price and volatility moving opposite. Reversal or slowdown likely.

required.title: Chaikin Volatility Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Chaikin Volatility Works
recommended.content: Chaikin Volatility calculates the percentage change in the high-low range over time using two EMAs. It measures the rate of volatility change rather than absolute volatility levels. When the indicator rises, volatility is accelerating. When it falls, volatility is decelerating. This helps identify periods of volatility expansion (breakouts) and contraction (squeezes).

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Chaikin Volatility Calculation
items: Calculate High-Low range for each period
Calculate EMA of range over short period (10)
Calculate EMA of range over long period (20)
Calculate percentage difference: (Short - Long) / Long × 100
Positive = volatility expanding
Negative = volatility contracting

items.name: Breakout Anticipation Strategy
items.description: When Chaikin Volatility is at extreme lows, expect a breakout. Prepare to enter in direction of breakout with volatility confirmation.

items.name: Volatility Expansion Strategy
items.description: Enter when Chaikin Volatility starts rising from low levels. Volatility expansion often leads to sustained moves. Ride the momentum.

items.name: Volatility + Breakout Strategy
items.description: Combine with Bollinger Bands. When bands squeeze and Chaikin Volatility is low, expect breakout. Enter when Chaikin Volatility confirms expansion.

items.name: Divergence Strategy
items.description: Price making new highs with falling Chaikin Volatility = bearish divergence. Price making new lows with rising Chaikin Volatility = bullish divergence.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
pros: Works with Bollinger Bands and Keltner
cons: Can be noisy in choppy markets

optional.title: History
optional.content: Chaikin Volatility was developed by Marc Chaikin as a companion to his money flow indicators. Chaikin recognized that volatility expansion often preceded significant price moves. The indicator helps traders anticipate breakouts by measuring the acceleration of volatility rather than just its absolute level.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: Chaikin Volatility works in all market conditions but is most useful during volatility transitions. It excels at identifying periods of volatility contraction (squeezes) and expansion (breakouts). Use with Bollinger Bands or Keltner Channels for confirmation.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using Chaikin Volatility alone without price confirmation
Ignoring the rate of change - focus on direction, not absolute values
Using in extremely low volatility without context
Expecting immediate breakouts after low readings
Using default settings without testing

faq.q: What is the best Chaikin Volatility setting for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, (10,20) works for swing trading. For day trading, use (5,10). For Bitcoin, (10,20) is standard. Altcoins with higher volatility may need (7,14).

faq.q: How to use Chaikin Volatility with ATR?
faq.a: Use ATR for absolute volatility levels and Chaikin Volatility for volatility rate of change. When both rising, volatility is expanding strongly. When both falling, volatility is contracting.

faq.q: What is the difference between Chaikin Volatility and ATR?
faq.a: ATR measures absolute volatility (how much price moves). Chaikin Volatility measures the rate of change of volatility (how fast volatility is changing). They complement each other.

meta.title: DPO - Detrended Price Oscillator: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn DPO formula, cycle identification, price analysis, trend removal, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: DPO: Complete Detrended Price Oscillator Guide

aeo.direct_answer: DPO (Detrended Price Oscillator) removes trend to reveal price cycles and overbought/oversold levels. Positive DPO indicates price above its detrended average, negative indicates below. DPO helps identify cyclical price patterns.
key_takeaways: Removes long-term trend
Reveals price cycles
Positive = above detrended average
Negative = below detrended average

people_also_ask.question: What is the best DPO period for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard DPO uses 20 periods. Day traders often use 10 periods for faster cycles. Swing traders use 20 periods standard.

people_also_ask.question: How to use DPO with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use DPO for cycle identification and RSI for momentum confirmation. When DPO turns positive and RSI shows bullish momentum, it's a buy signal.

people_also_ask.question: What does DPO tell you that price doesn't?
people_also_ask.a: DPO shows the cyclical component of price by removing the long-term trend. It reveals overbought/oversold conditions within the cycle that aren't visible in raw price.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: DPO removes trend to show price cycles. Think of it as price with the noise of long-term trends stripped away. Above zero = price is above its cycle average (bullish). Below zero = price is below its cycle average (bearish). DPO helps identify cyclical overbought/oversold conditions.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
DPO = Price - SMA(Price, N/2 + 1)
Where:
Price = Current closing price
N = Period (standard 20)
SMA = Simple Moving Average
DPO is shifted forward by N/2 + 1 periods to align with price

rows.meaning: Price above detrended average - bullish cycle phase.
Price below detrended average - bearish cycle phase.
Bullish cycle start - price moving above average. Potential buy.
Bearish cycle start - price moving below average. Potential sell.
Overbought or oversold within cycle. Potential reversal.

recommended.title: How DPO Works
recommended.content: DPO removes the long-term trend component from price, leaving only the cyclical component. By subtracting a shifted SMA from current price, DPO reveals when price is above or below its trend-adjusted average. This helps identify cyclical patterns and overbought/oversold conditions that are hidden by the trend. The indicator is shifted forward to align with price action.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step DPO Calculation
items: Calculate SMA of closing prices over N periods
Shift SMA forward by N/2 + 1 periods
Subtract shifted SMA from current price
Positive = bullish cycle, negative = bearish cycle
Watch for zero line crossovers for cycle changes
Use extremes for cycle timing

items.name: Zero Line Crossover Strategy
items.description: Buy when DPO crosses above zero (cycle turns bullish). Sell when DPO crosses below zero (cycle turns bearish).

items.name: Cycle Extreme Strategy
items.description: Buy when DPO reaches extreme lows (oversold). Sell when DPO reaches extreme highs (overbought). Mean reversion within cycle.

items.name: DPO + Trend Strategy
items.description: Use EMA for trend direction and DPO for cycle timing. Enter when DPO turns positive in uptrend. Exit when DPO turns negative.

items.name: DPO Divergence Strategy
items.description: Bullish divergence: price lower low, DPO higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, DPO lower high. Cycle divergence signals reversals.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
cons: Can be noisy in choppy markets

optional.title: History
optional.content: DPO was developed to help traders identify price cycles by removing the trend component. The concept of detrending prices has been used in technical analysis for decades. DPO gained popularity as cycle analysis became more common in trading.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: DPO works best in markets with clear price cycles. In strongly trending markets, DPO may stay on one side of zero for extended periods. Use with trend indicators to distinguish between cyclical moves and trend moves.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using DPO alone without trend context
Expecting cycles to repeat perfectly
Using wrong period for timeframe
Trading every zero line crossover without confirmation
Ignoring divergence signals

faq.q: What is the best DPO period for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, 20-period DPO works for swing trading. For day trading, use 10-period. For Bitcoin, 20-period is standard. Altcoins with faster cycles may need 14-period.

faq.q: How to use DPO with MACD?
faq.a: Use MACD for momentum and DPO for cycle timing. When both bullish, cycle and momentum align. DPO divergence with MACD gives strong reversal signals.

faq.q: What is the shift in DPO formula?
faq.a: DPO is shifted forward by N/2 + 1 periods. This aligns the oscillator with price action, making it easier to compare DPO signals with current price movements.

meta.title: KST - Know Sure Thing: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn KST Oscillator formula, multi-timeframe momentum, crossover signals, divergence patterns, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: KST: Complete Know Sure Thing Oscillator Guide

aeo.direct_answer: KST (Know Sure Thing) is a multi-timeframe momentum oscillator that combines four different timeframes. It smooths rate of change across multiple periods to identify market momentum and potential reversals.
key_takeaways: Combines four timeframes of momentum
Reduces false signals
Crossover of signal line = entry
Divergence signals are highly reliable

people_also_ask.question: What are the best KST settings for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard settings are ROC(10,15,20,30) with SMA(10,10,10,15). Day traders often use faster settings like (5,10,15,20). Swing traders use standard settings.

people_also_ask.question: How is KST different from MACD?
people_also_ask.a: KST uses multiple timeframes and rate of change calculations. MACD uses exponential moving averages of price. KST is more focused on momentum across timeframes.

people_also_ask.question: What does KST divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence: price lower low, KST higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, KST lower high. Both signal potential reversals with high reliability.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: KST combines momentum from four timeframes into one powerful oscillator. Think of it as MACD on steroids. Crossovers of KST with its signal line give entry signals. Multi-timeframe approach reduces false signals significantly.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
ROC1 = Rate of Change (10) smoothed by SMA(10)
ROC2 = Rate of Change (15) smoothed by SMA(10)
ROC3 = Rate of Change (20) smoothed by SMA(10)
ROC4 = Rate of Change (30) smoothed by SMA(15)
KST = ROC1 + 2×ROC2 + 3×ROC3 + 4×ROC4
Signal Line = SMA(KST, 9)

rows.meaning: Bullish crossover - momentum turning up. Potential buy.
Bearish crossover - momentum turning down. Potential sell.
Multi-timeframe momentum bullish. Uptrend likely.
Multi-timeframe momentum bearish. Downtrend likely.
Price and KST moving opposite. Reversal likely.

recommended.title: How KST Works
recommended.content: KST calculates rate of change across four different timeframes (10, 15, 20, 30 periods). Each ROC is smoothed and weighted - shorter timeframes get lower weights, longer timeframes get higher weights. The weighted sum creates the KST line. A 9-period SMA of KST creates the signal line. Crossovers provide entry signals, and divergence signals potential reversals.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step KST Calculation
items: Calculate ROC for 10, 15, 20, and 30 periods
Smooth each ROC with SMA (10,10,10,15)
Apply weights: 1×ROC1 + 2×ROC2 + 3×ROC3 + 4×ROC4
Result is KST line
Calculate 9-period SMA of KST as signal line
Watch crossovers and divergences

items.name: Crossover Strategy
items.description: Buy when KST crosses above signal line. Sell when KST crosses below signal line. Multi-timeframe confirmation.

items.name: Zero Line Strategy
items.description: Buy when KST crosses above zero (bullish). Sell when KST crosses below zero (bearish). Trend direction confirmation.

items.name: Divergence Strategy
items.description: Bullish divergence: price lower low, KST higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, KST lower high. High probability signals.

items.name: KST + Trend Strategy
items.description: Use with ADX for trend confirmation. Trade KST signals only when ADX > 25. Avoid signals in sideways markets.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
cons: Can be noisy in choppy markets

optional.title: History
optional.content: KST was developed by Martin Pring in 1992. Pring, a renowned technical analyst and author, created KST to identify market momentum across multiple timeframes. The name 'Know Sure Thing' reflects Pring's confidence in the indicator's ability to identify trend changes with high reliability.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: KST works best in trending markets with clear momentum across timeframes. In sideways markets, crossovers can be noisy. Combine with ADX for best results.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Trading every crossover without confirmation
Ignoring divergence signals
Using KST alone without trend context
Using default settings without testing
Expecting KST to work in all conditions

faq.q: What is the best KST setting for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, standard (10,15,20,30) works. For day trading, use (5,10,15,20). For Bitcoin, standard settings. Altcoins may need faster settings for higher volatility.

faq.q: How to use KST with MACD?
faq.a: Use MACD for trend and KST for momentum confirmation. When both bullish, trend is strong. KST diverging from MACD signals potential reversals.

faq.q: What are the weights in KST calculation?
faq.a: Weights are: ROC10 × 1, ROC15 × 2, ROC20 × 3, ROC30 × 4. This gives more importance to longer-term momentum while still including short-term information.

meta.title: Mass Index - Reversal Pattern: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Mass Index formula, reversal identification, volatility pattern analysis, bullish/bearish signals, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Mass Index: Complete Reversal Pattern Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Mass Index identifies trend reversals by measuring the widening and narrowing of the high-low range. Readings above 27 suggest a reversal may be imminent. When the Mass Index rises above 27 and then falls below 26.5, a trend reversal signal is generated.
key_takeaways: Above 27 = reversal warning
Falling below 26.5 = reversal signal
Measures volatility pattern
Identifies potential trend changes

people_also_ask.question: What is the best Mass Index setting for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard settings are 9-period and 25-period EMAs. Day traders often use shorter periods. Swing traders use standard settings.

people_also_ask.question: How to use Mass Index with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use Mass Index for reversal identification and RSI for momentum confirmation. When Mass Index signals reversal and RSI shows divergence, the signal is stronger.

people_also_ask.question: What does Mass Index measure?
people_also_ask.a: Mass Index measures the difference between two EMAs of the high-low range. It identifies when volatility is expanding and contracting, which often precedes reversals.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Mass Index identifies when a reversal is likely by measuring volatility patterns. Think of it as a reversal warning system. When Mass Index rises above 27 and then falls back below 26.5, a trend reversal is likely. It's a unique indicator that focuses on the pattern of volatility expansion and contraction.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Range = High - Low
EMA1 = EMA(Range, 9)
EMA2 = EMA(EMA1, 9)
Mass Index = SUM(EMA1 / EMA2, 25)
Reversal Signal: Mass Index > 27 then falls below 26.5

rows.meaning: Reversal warning - volatility pattern suggesting potential trend change.

rows.signal: Mass Index rises above 27 then falls below 26.5
rows.meaning: Reversal signal - bullish or bearish reversal likely. Confirm with price action.
Volatility expanding - wider ranges. Market becoming more active.
Volatility contracting - narrower ranges. Market quieting.
Normal volatility - no reversal signal. Trend likely continuing.

required.title: Mass Index Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Mass Index Works
recommended.content: Mass Index calculates the ratio of two EMAs of the high-low range. When volatility expands and contracts in a specific pattern, the indicator rises above 27. When it then falls below 26.5, a reversal signal is generated. The indicator is unique because it focuses on the pattern of volatility change rather than price direction.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Mass Index Calculation
items: Calculate High-Low range for each period
Calculate 9-period EMA of range
Calculate 9-period EMA of the EMA (double smoothed)
Calculate ratio: EMA1 / EMA2
Sum the ratio over 25 periods
Watch for >27 then fall below 26.5 for reversal signals

items.name: Reversal Signal Strategy
items.description: Wait for Mass Index to rise above 27 then fall below 26.5. This is the reversal signal. Enter in direction of trend reversal confirmed by price action.

items.name: Mass Index + Price Action Strategy
items.description: When reversal signal occurs, look for candlestick confirmation. Bullish reversal signal + bullish candlestick = buy. Bearish + bearish candlestick = sell.

items.name: Mass Index + RSI Strategy
items.description: Use Mass Index for timing and RSI for momentum. Reversal signal with RSI oversold/overbought is stronger. High probability setup.

items.name: Mass Index + Support/Resistance Strategy
items.description: Reversal signal at support or resistance levels is stronger. Combine with key levels for higher probability trades.

optional.title: History
optional.content: Mass Index was developed by Donald Dorsey in the 1990s. Dorsey created it to identify trend reversals by analyzing the expansion and contraction of price ranges. The indicator is based on the observation that reversals are often preceded by specific volatility patterns.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: Mass Index works in all market conditions but is most useful during periods of volatility transition. It excels at identifying reversals in both trending and ranging markets. Use with price action for best results.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using Mass Index alone without confirmation
Expecting reversals immediately after signals
Ignoring price action confirmation
Using wrong EMA periods
Trading every signal without context

faq.q: What is the best Mass Index setting for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, standard (9,25,27) works. For day trading, use shorter periods. For Bitcoin, standard settings. Altcoins may need adjusted thresholds due to higher volatility.

faq.q: How to use Mass Index with Bollinger Bands?
faq.a: Use Bollinger Bands for volatility context and Mass Index for reversal timing. When Bollinger Bands are wide and Mass Index signals reversal, expect a move back to the mean.

faq.q: What does the 27 threshold mean in Mass Index?
faq.a: The 27 threshold was derived from statistical analysis of volatility patterns. Readings above 27 indicate unusual volatility expansion that often precedes reversals. The 26.5 level confirms the reversal pattern is complete.

meta.title: Price ROC - Rate of Change: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Price ROC formula, momentum measurement, overbought/oversold signals, divergence patterns, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Price ROC: Complete Rate of Change Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Price ROC (Rate of Change) measures the percentage change in price over a specific period. Positive ROC indicates upward momentum, negative ROC indicates downward momentum. ROC helps identify trend strength and potential reversals.
key_takeaways: Positive ROC = upward momentum
Negative ROC = downward momentum
Extreme readings suggest overbought/oversold
Divergence signals potential reversals

people_also_ask.question: What is the best ROC period for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Day traders often use 5-10 period ROC for fast signals. Swing traders use 14-20 period ROC for smoother readings. Standard is 14 periods.

people_also_ask.question: How to use ROC with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use ROC for momentum and RSI for overbought/oversold confirmation. When ROC shows strong momentum and RSI confirms, signals are more reliable.

people_also_ask.question: What does ROC divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence: price lower low, ROC higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, ROC lower high. Both signal potential reversals.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Price ROC measures how fast price is changing. Think of it as a speedometer for price movement. Above zero = price moving up (bullish). Below zero = price moving down (bearish). Extreme readings suggest price may be overextended.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
ROC = ((Close - Close_N) / Close_N) × 100
Where:
Close = Current closing price
Close_N = Closing price N periods ago
N = 14 periods (standard)

rows.meaning: Bullish momentum - price moving up. Buyers in control.
Bearish momentum - price moving down. Sellers in control.
Overbought - price moving up too fast. Potential pullback.
Oversold - price moving down too fast. Potential bounce.
Price and ROC moving opposite. Reversal likely.

required.title: Price ROC Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Price ROC Works
recommended.content: Price ROC compares the current price to the price N periods ago as a percentage. It's a simple but powerful momentum indicator. Positive values indicate price is rising, negative values indicate price is falling. The magnitude shows the speed of change. ROC is often used as a leading indicator for trend changes.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Price ROC Calculation
items: Select period N (typically 14)
Find closing price N periods ago
Calculate: (Current Close - Close_N) / Close_N
Multiply by 100 for percentage
Positive = bullish, negative = bearish
Watch for extreme readings and divergences

items.name: Extreme Reversal Strategy

items.name: ROC Divergence Strategy

optional.title: History
optional.content: Rate of Change has been used in technical analysis for decades. It's one of the oldest momentum indicators, predating RSI and MACD. ROC was originally used in commodities trading before being applied to stocks and other markets.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: ROC works best in trending markets where momentum is clear. In sideways markets, ROC can give false signals. Use with ADX to confirm trend strength.

faq.q: What is the difference between ROC and momentum?
faq.a: ROC shows percentage change. Momentum shows absolute price difference. ROC is more comparable across different price levels.

Positive TRIX = bullish momentum
Negative TRIX = bearish momentum
Zero line crossover = trend change

people_also_ask.question: What is the best TRIX period for day trading?

people_also_ask.question: How to use TRIX with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use TRIX for trend direction and RSI for momentum confirmation. When TRIX bullish and RSI oversold, it's a strong buy signal.

people_also_ask.question: What does TRIX divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence: price lower low, TRIX higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, TRIX lower high. Both signal potential reversals.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: TRIX is a momentum indicator that applies triple exponential smoothing to filter out noise. Think of it as a cleaner version of ROC. Above zero = bullish momentum. Below zero = bearish momentum. Crossovers give trend change signals with reduced false signals.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
EMA1 = EMA(Close, N)
EMA2 = EMA(EMA1, N)
EMA3 = EMA(EMA2, N)
TRIX = (EMA3 - EMA3_Prev) / EMA3_Prev × 100
Where:
N = 15 periods (standard)
EMA = Exponential Moving Average

rows.meaning: Bullish momentum - triple-smoothed trend up. Uptrend confirmed.
Bearish momentum - triple-smoothed trend down. Downtrend confirmed.

Bearish crossover - trend turning down. Potential sell signal.
Price and TRIX moving opposite. Reversal likely.

recommended.title: How TRIX Works
recommended.content: TRIX applies exponential smoothing three times to price data. This triple smoothing filters out short-term noise and reveals the underlying trend. The final value shows the percentage change of the triple-smoothed EMA. TRIX is essentially a smoothed Rate of Change that is less sensitive to market noise than standard ROC.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step TRIX Calculation
items: Calculate 15-period EMA of closing prices
Calculate 15-period EMA of the first EMA
Calculate 15-period EMA of the second EMA (triple smoothed)
Calculate percentage change of triple-smoothed EMA
Positive = bullish, negative = bearish
Watch for zero line crossovers and divergences

optional.content: TRIX was developed by Jack Hutson in the 1980s. Hutson, an editor at Technical Analysis of Stocks & Commodities, created TRIX to filter out market noise. The triple smoothing approach makes it less sensitive to short-term fluctuations than other momentum indicators.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: TRIX works best in trending markets where noise filtering is beneficial. In sideways markets, TRIX stays near zero and gives few signals. Use with ADX for best results.

faq.q: What is the difference between TRIX and ROC?
faq.a: TRIX applies triple exponential smoothing to price before calculating rate of change. ROC uses raw price. TRIX is smoother with fewer false signals.

seo_schema.headline: Typical Price: Complete Average Price Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Typical Price is the average of high, low, and close prices. It provides a single representative price for the trading period. Used as input for many indicators including Money Flow Index and Accumulation Distribution.
key_takeaways: Average of High, Low, and Close
Single representative price
Used in many indicators
Reduces intraday noise

people_also_ask.question: What is Typical Price used for?
people_also_ask.a: Typical Price is used as input for many volume-based indicators like Money Flow Index, Accumulation Distribution, and Chaikin Money Flow.

people_also_ask.question: How is Typical Price different from Close?
people_also_ask.a: Typical Price includes high and low prices, giving a more complete picture of the day's trading range. Close only uses the closing price.

people_also_ask.question: Is Typical Price better than Close?
people_also_ask.a: Typical Price is better for volume-weighted indicators because it considers the entire trading range. Close is better for trend following indicators.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Typical Price is the average of high, low, and close. Think of it as a day's average price that accounts for the entire trading range. It's not a standalone trading indicator but a building block for other indicators like MFI and A/D Line.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Typical Price = (High + Low + Close) / 3
Where:
High = Highest price of the period
Low = Lowest price of the period
Close = Closing price of the period

rows.meaning: Price trading above the daily average - bullish bias for the day.
Price trading below the daily average - bearish bias for the day.

Input for Accumulation Distribution - volume flow analysis.

items: Find the High price for the period
Find the Low price for the period
Find the Close price for the period
Add all three together
Divide by 3

meta.title: Standard Deviation - Volatility: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Standard Deviation formula, volatility measurement, Bollinger Bands input, market analysis, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Standard Deviation: Complete Volatility Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Standard Deviation measures the dispersion of price from its average. Higher values indicate higher volatility, lower values indicate lower volatility. It's the foundation of Bollinger Bands and many statistical trading models.
key_takeaways: Measures price dispersion from average
Higher = more volatile
Lower = less volatile
Input for Bollinger Bands

people_also_ask.question: What is a good Standard Deviation value for trading?
people_also_ask.a: There's no fixed good value as it varies by market. Compare current Standard Deviation to its historical average. Higher than average = high volatility, lower = low volatility.

people_also_ask.question: How is Standard Deviation used in Bollinger Bands?
people_also_ask.a: Bollinger Bands use Standard Deviation to set band width. Upper band = SMA + (2 × StdDev). Lower band = SMA - (2 × StdDev). Wider bands = higher volatility.

people_also_ask.question: Is Standard Deviation the same as volatility?
people_also_ask.a: Standard Deviation is the most common measure of volatility in finance. Higher standard deviation means higher volatility and greater price uncertainty.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Standard Deviation measures how spread out prices are from the average. Think of it as a volatility meter. Higher values = prices are spread out (volatile). Lower values = prices are clustered (quiet). It's the engine behind Bollinger Bands.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Mean = Sum(Price) / N
Variance = Sum((Price - Mean)^2) / N
Standard Deviation = √Variance
Where:
N = 20 periods (standard)
Price = Closing price (or any price series)

rows.meaning: Volatility expanding - bigger price swings ahead. Prepare for breakouts.
Volatility contracting - smaller price swings. Squeeze building.
High volatility regime - larger swings. Expect more price movement.
Low volatility regime - quiet market. Potential breakout approaching.
Compare to historical average. Above = volatile, below = quiet.

required.title: Standard Deviation Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Standard Deviation Works
recommended.content: Standard Deviation calculates how much prices deviate from their average. In a normal distribution, about 68% of prices fall within one standard deviation of the mean, 95% within two, and 99.7% within three. This statistical property makes Standard Deviation valuable for setting volatility-based price bands like Bollinger Bands.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Standard Deviation Calculation
items: Calculate average (mean) of prices over N periods
Subtract mean from each price (deviation)
Square each deviation
Average the squared deviations (variance)
Take square root of variance
Result is Standard Deviation for the period

items.name: Volatility Regime Strategy
items.description: Trade breakouts when Standard Deviation is rising from low levels. Trade mean-reversion when Standard Deviation is at extreme highs. Adapt strategy to volatility regime.

items.name: Bollinger Bands Strategy
items.description: Use Standard Deviation to set Bollinger Bands. Buy at lower band when StdDev is expanding. Sell at upper band when StdDev is contracting.

items.name: Volatility Breakout Strategy
items.description: When Standard Deviation reaches low levels (volatility squeeze), prepare for breakout. Enter when price breaks out with expanding StdDev.

items.name: Standard Deviation + RSI Strategy
items.description: Use StdDev for volatility context and RSI for momentum. High StdDev + RSI overbought = strong reversal potential. Low StdDev + RSI divergence = trend change.

optional.title: History
optional.content: Standard Deviation has been used in statistics since the 18th century. In finance, it was popularized by modern portfolio theory in the 1950s. John Bollinger later used it as the basis for Bollinger Bands in the 1980s.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: Standard Deviation is useful in all market conditions. It measures volatility regardless of market regime. Use it to adapt your strategy to changing volatility conditions.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using Standard Deviation alone for entries
Assuming normal distribution in markets
Using same period for all markets
Ignoring trend direction with volatility
Using Standard Deviation without other indicators

faq.q: What is the best Standard Deviation period for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, 20-period StdDev is standard for swing trading. For day trading, use 10-period. For Bitcoin, 20-period works well. Altcoins may need 14-period.

faq.q: How to use Standard Deviation with Bollinger Bands?
faq.a: Standard Deviation sets the bandwidth in Bollinger Bands. Multiply StdDev by 2 for upper/lower bands. The bands widen with higher StdDev (volatility expansion) and narrow with lower StdDev (volatility contraction).

faq.q: What is the difference between Standard Deviation and ATR?
faq.a: Standard Deviation measures dispersion from the average. ATR measures average high-low range. StdDev is better for statistical analysis. ATR is better for stop loss placement.

meta.title: Ease of Movement - EOM: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Ease of Movement formula, volume-price relationship, trend strength analysis, crossover signals, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Ease of Movement: Complete EOM Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Ease of Movement (EOM) measures the relationship between price movement and volume. High positive EOM indicates price moving up easily on low volume, high negative indicates price moving down easily. EOM helps identify trend strength and potential reversals.
key_takeaways: Positive EOM = easy upward movement
Negative EOM = easy downward movement
Uses volume to confirm price moves
Zero line crossover = trend change

people_also_ask.question: What is the best EOM period for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard EOM uses 14-period. Day traders often use 7-period for faster signals. Swing traders use 14-period standard.

people_also_ask.question: How to use EOM with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use EOM for trend strength and RSI for momentum confirmation. When EOM positive and RSI oversold, it's a strong buy signal.

people_also_ask.question: What does EOM divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence: price lower low, EOM higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, EOM lower high. Both signal potential reversals.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Ease of Movement shows how easily price moves with volume. Think of it as a smoothness indicator - high positive means price moves up easily (no resistance). High negative means price moves down easily (no support). Zero line crossovers signal trend changes.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Midpoint = (High + Low) / 2
Box Ratio = (High - Low) / Volume
Distance = Midpoint - Midpoint_Prev
EOM = Distance / Box Ratio
EOM_Smoothed = SMA(EOM, N)
Where N = 14 periods (standard)

rows.meaning: Easy upward movement - price moving up with low resistance. Bullish.
Easy downward movement - price moving down with low support. Bearish.
Bullish crossover - price moving up easily. Potential buy.
Bearish crossover - price moving down easily. Potential sell.
Price and EOM moving opposite. Reversal likely.

required.title: Ease of Movement Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Ease of Movement Works
recommended.content: EOM measures the relationship between price movement and volume. It calculates how much price moves relative to the volume required to achieve that move. When price moves up on low volume, EOM is positive (easy movement). When price moves down on low volume, EOM is negative. When high volume is required to move price, EOM is near zero (difficult movement).

recommended.title: Step-by-Step EOM Calculation
items: Calculate midpoint: (High + Low) / 2
Calculate distance: Midpoint - Previous Midpoint
Calculate box ratio: (High - Low) / Volume
EOM = Distance / Box Ratio
Smooth EOM with SMA over N periods
Positive = easy up, negative = easy down

items.description: Buy when EOM crosses above zero. Sell when EOM crosses below zero. Trend change signal.

optional.content: EOM works best in liquid markets with consistent volume. In low volume markets, EOM can be unreliable. Use with ADX for trend confirmation.

Using wrong period for timeframe

faq.a: Force Index uses price change × volume. MFI uses typical price and volume ratio. Force Index is simpler, MFI has overbought/oversold levels.

meta.title: MFI - Money Flow Index: Formula & Signals
meta.description: Learn MFI formula, volume-weighted momentum, overbought/oversold signals, divergence patterns, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: MFI: Complete Money Flow Index Guide

aeo.direct_answer: MFI (Money Flow Index) is a volume-weighted RSI that measures buying and selling pressure. Readings above 80 indicate overbought conditions, below 20 indicate oversold conditions. MFI combines price and volume to identify money flow strength.
key_takeaways: Above 80 = overbought (potential sell)
Below 20 = oversold (potential buy)
Volume-weighted - more reliable than RSI
Divergence signals strong reversals

people_also_ask.question: What is the best MFI period for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard MFI uses 14 periods. Day traders often use 10-period MFI for faster signals. Swing traders prefer 14-period standard or 20-period for smoother readings.

people_also_ask.question: How is MFI different from RSI?
people_also_ask.a: MFI includes volume in its calculation while RSI only uses price. MFI is more reliable because it shows whether money is flowing in or out. MFI signals are generally more accurate.

people_also_ask.question: What does MFI divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence (price lower low, MFI higher low) - money flowing in despite lower prices, reversal up likely. Bearish divergence (price higher high, MFI lower high) - money flowing out despite higher prices, reversal down likely.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: MFI is RSI with volume added. Think of it as a smart momentum indicator that knows when money is actually flowing. Above 80 = overbought (money flowing in too fast). Below 20 = oversold (money flowing out too fast). MFI signals are more reliable because they include volume.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
Typical Price = (High + Low + Close) / 3
Money Flow = Typical Price × Volume
Money Ratio = Positive Money Flow / Negative Money Flow
MFI = 100 - (100 / (1 + Money Ratio))
Where:
Positive MF = Money Flow on up days
Negative MF = Money Flow on down days
Standard period = 14

rows.meaning: Strong buying pressure - money flowing in. Potential top or pullback.
Strong selling pressure - money flowing out. Potential bottom or bounce.
Bullish - money flowing back in. Potential buy signal.
Bearish - money flowing out. Potential sell signal.
Price and MFI moving opposite. Money flow diverging from price. Reversal likely.

recommended.title: How MFI Works
recommended.content: MFI calculates money flow by multiplying price by volume. It then compares positive money flow (up days) to negative money flow (down days). This ratio is converted to a 0-100 scale. MFI is essentially RSI with volume weighting, making it more reliable for identifying genuine buying and selling pressure.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step MFI Calculation
items: Calculate Typical Price for each period
Calculate Money Flow: Typical Price × Volume
Identify positive and negative money flow periods
Sum positive money flow and negative money flow
Calculate Money Ratio: Positive / Negative
Calculate MFI: 100 - (100 / (1 + Money Ratio))

items.name: Overbought/Oversold Strategy
items.description: Buy when MFI crosses above 20 (leaving oversold). Sell when MFI crosses below 80 (leaving overbought). Includes volume confirmation.

items.name: MFI Divergence Strategy
items.description: Bullish divergence (price lower low, MFI higher low) with MFI oversold - strong buy. Bearish divergence (price higher high, MFI lower high) with MFI overbought - strong sell.

items.name: MFI + Volume Strategy
items.description: Use volume to confirm MFI signals. MFI signal with increasing volume = stronger. MFI signal with decreasing volume = weaker.

items.name: MFI + RSI Strategy
items.description: Use both indicators for confirmation. When RSI and MFI align on overbought/oversold, signals are much stronger. Divergence on both is very reliable.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
pros: Volume-weighted - more reliable than price-only indicators
Shows actual money flow, not just price momentum
Works across all markets and timeframes
cons: Can stay overbought/oversold in strong trends
Less effective in low volume periods

optional.title: History
optional.content: MFI was developed by Gene Quong and Avrum Soudack in the 1990s as a volume-weighted version of RSI. They recognized that volume provides crucial context for momentum readings. MFI has become popular among traders who want to confirm price moves with volume data.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: MFI works best in liquid markets with consistent volume. It's particularly effective in stocks and crypto where volume data is reliable. Use ADX to confirm trend strength - MFI signals are more reliable when ADX > 25.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using MFI alone without price confirmation
Trading every overbought/oversold signal without context
Ignoring divergence signals - the most reliable MFI setup
Using MFI on low volume markets
Expecting MFI to work without volume data

faq.q: What is the best MFI setting for crypto trading?
faq.a: For crypto, 14-period MFI works well for swing trading. For day trading, use 10-period for faster signals. Bitcoin works well with 14-period, altcoins with higher volatility may need 10-period.

faq.q: How to use MFI with OBV?
faq.a: Use MFI for momentum and OBV for volume trend confirmation. When MFI overbought and OBV diverging down, money flowing out despite high price - bearish. Use both for stronger signals.

faq.q: What is the difference between MFI and OBV?
faq.a: MFI shows momentum with overbought/oversold levels (0-100 scale). OBV shows cumulative volume flow direction without overbought/oversold levels. MFI for entries, OBV for trend confirmation.

meta.title: CMO - Chande Momentum Oscillator: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn CMO formula, momentum measurement, overbought/oversold signals, divergence patterns, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: CMO: Complete Chande Momentum Oscillator Guide

aeo.direct_answer: CMO (Chande Momentum Oscillator) measures momentum by comparing the sum of upward price changes to downward price changes. Readings above +50 indicate strong bullish momentum, below -50 indicate strong bearish momentum. CMO is similar to RSI but more responsive.
key_takeaways: Above +50 = strong bullish momentum
Below -50 = strong bearish momentum
More responsive than RSI
Divergence signals potential reversals

people_also_ask.question: What is the best CMO period for day trading?
people_also_ask.a: Standard CMO uses 14 periods. Day traders often use 9-period for faster signals. Swing traders use 14-period standard or 20-period for smoother readings.

people_also_ask.question: How is CMO different from RSI?
people_also_ask.a: CMO uses the sum of gains and losses divided by their total. RSI uses the ratio of average gains to average losses. CMO is more responsive and has wider range (-100 to +100).

people_also_ask.question: What does CMO divergence indicate?
people_also_ask.a: Bullish divergence: price lower low, CMO higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, CMO lower high. Both signal potential trend reversals.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: CMO measures momentum by comparing up days to down days. Think of it as RSI's more responsive cousin. Above +50 = strong bullish momentum. Below -50 = strong bearish momentum. The wider range (-100 to +100) gives clearer signals than RSI.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
CMO = ((Sum of Up Changes - Sum of Down Changes) / (Sum of Up Changes + Sum of Down Changes)) × 100
Where:
Up Changes = Positive price changes
Down Changes = Absolute value of negative price changes
Standard period = 14
Range: -100 to +100

rows.meaning: Strong bullish momentum - uptrend likely. Potential overbought.
Strong bearish momentum - downtrend likely. Potential oversold.
Bullish crossover - momentum turning up. Potential buy.
Bearish crossover - momentum turning down. Potential sell.
Price and CMO moving opposite. Reversal likely ahead.

recommended.title: How CMO Works
recommended.content: CMO calculates the difference between the sum of upward and downward price changes, divided by their total sum. This creates an oscillator that ranges from -100 to +100. Positive values indicate bullish momentum, negative values indicate bearish momentum. CMO is similar to RSI but is more responsive and has a wider range.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step CMO Calculation
items: Calculate price changes for each period
Sum all positive (up) changes
Sum all negative (down) changes (absolute value)
CMO = (Up Sum - Down Sum) / (Up Sum + Down Sum) × 100
Positive = bullish, negative = bearish
Watch for extreme levels and divergences

items.name: Overbought/Oversold Strategy
items.description: Buy when CMO crosses above -50 (leaving oversold). Sell when CMO crosses below +50 (leaving overbought). More responsive than RSI.

items.name: Zero Line Crossover Strategy
items.description: Buy when CMO crosses above 0. Sell when CMO crosses below 0. Simple momentum shift signal.

items.name: CMO Divergence Strategy
items.description: Bullish divergence: price lower low, CMO higher low. Bearish divergence: price higher high, CMO lower high. High probability signals.

items.name: CMO + Trend Strategy
items.description: Use ADX for trend confirmation. Take CMO signals when ADX > 25. Avoid signals in sideways markets.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
pros: Wider range gives better signal clarity
cons: Can be noisy in choppy markets
Can stay extreme in strong trends

optional.title: History
optional.content: Chande Momentum Oscillator was developed by Tushar Chande in 1994. Chande, a technical analyst and author, created CMO as an improvement over RSI. He wanted an indicator that was more responsive and had a wider range for clearer signals.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: CMO works best in trending markets with clear momentum. In sideways markets, signals can be noisy. Use with ADX for trend confirmation.

optional.title: Common Mistakes
items: Using CMO alone without confirmation
Trading every overbought/oversold signal
Ignoring divergence signals
Using wrong period for timeframe
Expecting CMO to work in all conditions

faq.q: What is the best CMO period for crypto?
faq.a: For crypto, 14-period CMO works for swing trading. For day trading, use 9-period. For Bitcoin, 14-period is standard. Altcoins may need 10-period for faster signals.

faq.q: How to use CMO with MACD?
faq.a: Use MACD for trend and CMO for momentum confirmation. When both bullish, trend is strong. CMO divergence with MACD signals potential reversals.

faq.q: What is the difference between CMO and RSI?
faq.a: CMO uses the sum of gains and losses divided by their total. RSI uses the ratio of average gains to average losses. CMO ranges from -100 to +100, RSI from 0 to 100. CMO is more responsive.

meta.title: Coppock Curve - Long-Term: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn Coppock Curve formula, long-term momentum measurement, buy signal identification, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: Coppock Curve: Complete Long-Term Guide

aeo.direct_answer: Coppock Curve identifies long-term buying opportunities by combining momentum and trend. It's calculated as a 10-period WMA of the sum of 14-period and 11-period Rate of Change. Cross above zero generates a buy signal.
key_takeaways: Long-term buying opportunity indicator
Combines 14-period and 11-period ROC
Cross above zero = buy signal
Designed for long-term investors

people_also_ask.question: What is the best Coppock Curve setting for investing?
people_also_ask.a: Standard settings are (11,14,10) for ROC periods and WMA. These were designed for long-term investing on monthly charts.

people_also_ask.question: How to use Coppock Curve with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use Coppock for long-term entry timing and RSI for momentum confirmation. When Coppock turns positive and RSI bullish, it's a strong buy signal.

people_also_ask.question: What does Coppock Curve crossover indicate?
people_also_ask.a: When Coppock Curve crosses above zero from below, it generates a buy signal. This indicates that long-term momentum is turning positive.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: Coppock Curve is a long-term buying signal indicator. Think of it as a macro momentum gauge. When it crosses above zero, it's saying 'it's time to buy'. Designed for monthly charts and long-term investors. Simple, reliable, and time-tested.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
ROC_14 = Rate of Change over 14 periods
ROC_11 = Rate of Change over 11 periods
Sum = ROC_14 + ROC_11
Coppock Curve = WMA(Sum, 10)
Where:
ROC = Rate of Change
WMA = Weighted Moving Average
Cross above zero = Buy Signal

rows.meaning: Long-term buy signal. Major trend turning up. Time to accumulate.
Long-term bullish momentum. Uptrend confirmed.
Long-term bearish momentum. Downtrend confirmed.
Momentum improving. Potential bottom formation.
Momentum weakening. Potential top formation.

required.title: Coppock Curve Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How Coppock Curve Works
recommended.content: Coppock Curve uses two Rate of Change periods (14 and 11 months) to capture both short-term and medium-term momentum. These are summed and smoothed with a 10-period WMA. The indicator is designed for monthly data and was developed to identify long-term buying opportunities. The zero line crossover is the primary signal.

recommended.title: Step-by-Step Coppock Curve Calculation
items: Calculate 14-period ROC of closing prices
Calculate 11-period ROC of closing prices
Sum both ROCs
Calculate 10-period WMA of the sum
Result is the Coppock Curve
Cross above zero = buy signal

items.name: Buy Signal Strategy
items.description: Buy when Coppock Curve crosses above zero from below. Sell or wait when it turns negative. Long-term trend following.

recommended.title: Pros & Cons
pros: Simple buy signal for long-term investing
cons: Not useful for trading or scalping

optional.title: History
optional.content: Coppock Curve was developed by Edwin Coppock in 1965. Coppock was a San Antonio-based economist who created the indicator to identify long-term buying opportunities. The indicator was originally published in Barron's magazine. Coppock believed that bear markets ended with a specific momentum pattern.

optional.title: Best Market Conditions
optional.content: Coppock Curve is designed for all market conditions on monthly charts. It signals the end of bear markets and the start of bull markets. It's most effective for long-term asset allocation decisions.

items: Using Coppock on daily or weekly charts
Trading every signal without confirmation
Using Coppock for short-term trading

Using instead of other momentum indicators

faq.a: Coppock Curve is designed for monthly charts. Using it on weekly or daily charts reduces its effectiveness. The standard settings (11,14,10) were optimized for monthly data.

faq.q: How to use Coppock Curve with MACD?
faq.a: Use Coppock for entry timing on monthly charts and MACD for confirmation. When both show bullish momentum, it's a strong long-term buy signal.

faq.q: What does Coppock Curve tell you?
faq.a: Coppock Curve tells you when long-term momentum is turning positive. It's designed to identify the end of bear markets and the beginning of bull markets.

meta.title: DeMark Sequential - TD: Formula & Strategy
meta.description: Learn DeMark Sequential formula, TD setup, TD countdown, exhaustion signals, trend reversal identification, and get code in Python, Rust, Go, and 19 more languages.

seo_schema.headline: DeMark Sequential: Complete TD Guide

aeo.direct_answer: DeMark Sequential identifies trend exhaustion and potential reversals through TD Setup and TD Countdown phases. TD Setup signals momentum, TD Countdown indicates exhaustion. Completing 9 consecutive bars in Setup or 13 bars in Countdown suggests a reversal.
key_takeaways: TD Setup = 9 consecutive bars in same direction
TD Countdown = 13 bars after Setup completion
Signals trend exhaustion
Used for identifying reversals

people_also_ask.question: What is the DeMark Sequential setup?
people_also_ask.a: TD Setup requires 9 consecutive bars where each close is lower than the close 4 bars earlier (in downtrends) or higher than the close 4 bars earlier (in uptrends).

people_also_ask.question: How to use DeMark Sequential with RSI?
people_also_ask.a: Use DeMark Sequential for timing and RSI for momentum confirmation. When Sequential signals exhaustion and RSI shows divergence, reversal probability is high.

people_also_ask.question: What does TD Countdown indicate?
people_also_ask.a: TD Countdown is the second phase after Setup. It requires 13 bars with certain conditions. Completion suggests trend exhaustion and potential reversal.

required.title: Quick Answer
required.content: DeMark Sequential counts exhaustion bars. Think of it as a momentum odometer. TD Setup = 9 bars of directional momentum. TD Countdown = 13 more bars signaling exhaustion. When the sequence completes, reversal is likely. It's a timing tool for catching tops and bottoms.

required.title: Formula
required.content:
TD Setup (Uptrend): Close > Close_4 for 9 consecutive bars
TD Setup (Downtrend): Close < Close_4 for 9 consecutive bars
TD Countdown: After Setup, 13 bars with Close < Low_2 (buy) or Close > High_2 (sell)
Completion = Potential Reversal Signal

rows.meaning: Directional momentum confirmed. Trend may be nearing exhaustion.
Trend exhaustion signal - reversal likely. Time to prepare for turn.
Buying exhaustion - potential top. Consider selling or shorting.
Selling exhaustion - potential bottom. Consider buying.
Countdown with divergence = stronger reversal signal.

required.title: DeMark Sequential Code in Your Language

recommended.title: How DeMark Sequential Works
recommended.content: DeMark Sequential has two phases. First, TD Setup counts 9 consecutive bars in the same direction (each close higher than the close 4 bars earlier in uptrends, or lower in downtrends). Second, TD Countdown requires 13 bars after Setup with specific conditions. Completion suggests the trend is exhausted and a reversal is likely. The indicator is complex but powerful for identifying major turning points.

items: For TD Setup (Uptrend): Count consecutive bars where Close > Close_4
Setup completes at 9 bars

faq.a: Use DeMark Sequential for timing and Fibonacci for price levels. Countdown completion at Fibonacci retracement or extension levels gives high probability reversal signals.
